| 8:00 AM | Arrive at Woodstock Middle School. Find Mr. Roche. |
| 8:00-8:30 | **Setup:** Connect laptop to smart TV. Test screen, sound, Wi-Fi. Open ChatGPT and test it. Have demo prompts in a separate tab. |
| 8:30-8:50 | **Meet Kristy and Gina.** Introduce yourself. Ask what they're covering so you don't overlap. Ask how you can support their sessions. |
| 8:50-9:00 | **Stand at the front. Water bottle. Deep breath.** |
| 9:00-9:30 | **KEYNOTE** |
| 9:30-9:35 | Transition. Show QR code for toolkit. Let people scan. |
| 9:35-~11:00 | **Breakout sessions.** Float between Gina's and Kristy's rooms. |
| 11:00-2:30 | Stay as long as useful. 1-on-1 conversations with interested teachers. Get emails. |
| End of day | **Private conversation with Roche.** The seed. |

---

## The Keynote: 30 Minutes

The structure is: **WHO → WHY → WHAT I SAW → WHAT WE DID → WHAT YOU CAN DO → TOOLS**

You lead with your story. Your story leads into the fear. The fear leads into your why. Your why earns you the right to teach them. Then you teach them.

---

### Part 1: Who I Am (3 min)

**No slide needed. Just you talking.**

> "Good morning. My name is Brayan Tenesaca. I grew up in Danbury. I went to AIS Magnet School — Mr. Roche was actually my principal."

(Pause. Let the room react. Roche will smile. The room connects you to someone they know.)

> "I played soccer all four years of high school. Won five trophies. Scored over 100 goals. I now train mixed martial arts at Babson College, where I'm a freshman."

(This matters. You're not a "tech kid." You're an athlete, a competitor, a grinder. They need to see that before you talk about AI.)

> "I'm also the co-founder of Next Generation Learners. We run AI literacy programs for students in libraries and schools across Connecticut — Danbury, Ridgefield, Easton, and now here in Woodstock. I'm 18 years old."

(Let that land. 18. Founded a company. Running programs. In their building.)

---

### Part 2: The Fear — Why I'm Here (4 min)

**This is the emotional core of your entire talk. Slow down. Mean every word.**

> "When I got to Babson, I saw something that scared me. I saw students my age building entire businesses with AI — real revenue, real products — while other students were terrified to even open it. The gap was massive. And I realized: that gap doesn't start in college. It starts now. It starts in your classrooms."

> "So I started paying attention. And I saw fear everywhere."

> "I saw the fear of parents — worried their kids will never get exposed to AI, or worried they'll get TOO exposed to it without guidance."

> "I saw the fear of students — afraid they'll get caught using AI, not knowing there's a right way to use it."

> "I saw the fear of schools — that they won't adapt fast enough, that the world is moving and they're standing still."

> "And I saw the fear of teachers."

(Pause here. Look at them.)

> "I saw teachers afraid that AI will replace them. That their job — the job they pour their heart into — is somehow disposable. That a machine can do what they do."

(Pause again.)

> "And I thought: I have to do something about this. Because that fear? It's wrong. And I need you to hear me on this."

---

### Part 3: Why Teachers Matter (3 min)

**This is where you earn the room. Every teacher in that cafeteria needs to hear this.**

> "AI is not going to replace you. It will never replace you. Here's what it WILL do: it will replace people who refuse to learn how to use it. That's different. That's a choice, not a fate."

> "But let me tell you what AI can never do."

> "AI cannot care about your students. You can tell a machine what to do. You can tell it how to respond. But you cannot teach it to care. It doesn't stay after school because it noticed a kid was having a bad day. It doesn't throw a football with a 5th grader because that kid needed someone to show up for him."

(This is about Roche. He'll know. The room will feel it.)

> "No one can take away your teaching style. No one can take away the way you connect with a student who's about to give up. No one can replicate the moment a kid's eyes light up because YOU explained it in the way that finally made sense."

> "Teachers are often overlooked. People talk about your job like it's replaceable. But teaching is one of the most important jobs on earth. Our first teachers were our parents. Then you. You are the ones who pass wisdom from one generation to the next. You fuel the next generation. Without you, we don't have a society."

> "So no — AI is not here to replace you. AI is here to make the most important job in the world a little easier. So you can spend less time on the admin, and more time doing what only YOU can do: caring about your students."

(Let the room breathe. Don't rush into the next part.)

---

### Part 4: What We Found (3 min)

> "So we went and tested this. We ran a 4-session AI literacy program for 4th and 5th graders at the Danbury Library. And what we found surprised even us."

> "Students showed up thinking AI was magic — that it knows everything, that it's a brain. By session 4, those same kids were disagreeing with AI. They were revising their own prompts. They were explaining WHY they agreed or disagreed with the output."

> "Here's the insight that changed everything for us: AI literacy doesn't replace thinking. It makes thinking VISIBLE. For the first time, we could actually see what was happening inside a student's head — through their prompts, their revisions, their reflections."

> "And one more thing: the students who asked the worst questions learned the most. Because they saw the gap between a vague question and a specific one in real time. And that skill — asking better questions — transfers to everything. Not just AI. Life."

---

### Part 5: The Mindset — Partner vs Employee (2 min)

> "Here's the frame we teach every student, and it's the same one I want to leave you with today."

> "There are two ways to use AI. As an employee: 'Do this for me.' One prompt, one answer, done. That's what students do when nobody teaches them. That's what cheating looks like."

> "Or as a partner: 'Help me think.' Back-and-forth. Questions. Iteration. The human stays in control."

> "When we taught 4th graders this distinction, something clicked. They stopped asking AI for answers and started asking AI for better questions. That's the shift."

---

### Part 6: Live Demos — What This Looks Like For You (10 min)

> "Enough talking. Let me show you."

(Switch to ChatGPT. Run all 4 demos live.)

**Demo 1 — Lesson Plan Partner (3 min)**
```
I teach 6th grade ELA. I'm starting a unit on persuasive writing next week. Before you give me a lesson plan, ask me 5 questions about my students so you can personalize it.
```
SAY: "Notice I didn't ask for a lesson plan. I asked it to ask ME questions first. That's the partner mindset. Your expertise leads. AI supports."

**Demo 2 — Differentiation in 60 Seconds (3 min)**
```
Now adapt this for a student who understands the concepts but struggles with English as a second language. Keep the thinking demands high but simplify the language.
```
Then:
```
Now create a version for a student who finishes early and needs extension.
```
SAY: "60 seconds. Two differentiated versions. You made every decision. How many of your students know more than they can show you because of a language barrier? AI just removed that barrier. Not the rigor — the barrier."

**Demo 3 — Student Simulator (2 min)**
```
Act as a 7th grader who is confused about fractions. I'm going to explain it to you, and you tell me where my explanation is unclear or where a real student might get lost.
```
SAY: "Practice your lesson before class, not during it. Find the gaps before your students do."

**Demo 4 — Feedback Writer (2 min)**
```
Here's a paragraph a 5th grader wrote about their favorite animal: "Dogs are the best pets because they are fun and nice. They play with you and love you. I want a golden retriever." Give feedback the way a great teacher would — specific, encouraging, focused on one thing to improve. Don't rewrite it.
```
SAY: "Specific. Encouraging. One clear next step. And you decide if it sounds like YOU before it ever reaches the student. That's the partner mindset."

---

### Part 7: The Toolkit (2 min)

> "Everything I just showed you is yours. I built a toolkit with 6 of these AI drivers — copy, paste into ChatGPT, and use them today."

> "There's also a 15-minute AI lesson you can run with your students on Monday. Tested with real kids. No prep needed."

> "And 3 rules you can print and put on every desk. Rule 1: I think first. Rule 2: AI asks ME questions. Rule 3: I stay in control."

(Show QR code for `nextgenerationlearners.com/woodstock`)

> "Scan this. Everything is there. It's free. It's yours."

---

### Part 8: Close (2 min)

> "I started this because I saw fear. Fear from every direction. And I realized that fear doesn't go away by avoiding the thing you're afraid of. It goes away when someone shows you how to use it."

> "You are that someone for your students. You are the ones who will teach them to think with AI, not outsource their thinking to it. Nobody else is going to do that. Not an app. Not a YouTube video. You."

> "Your students are watching. Show them what's possible."

> "Gina and Kristy are going to take you deeper in the breakout sessions. I'll be there too. Come find me. I want to hear your questions, your concerns, and your ideas. Thank you."

**Total: ~29 min. 1 min buffer.**

---

### The 4 Live Demos (Have These in a Notes Doc Ready to Paste)

**Demo 1 — Lesson Plan Partner (3 min)**
```
I teach 6th grade ELA. I'm starting a unit on persuasive writing next week. Before you give me a lesson plan, ask me 5 questions about my students so you can personalize it.
```
SAY: "Notice I didn't ask for a lesson plan. I asked it to ask ME questions. That's the partner mindset."

**Demo 2 — Differentiation in 60 Seconds (3 min)**
```
Now adapt this for a student who understands the concepts but struggles with English as a second language. Keep the thinking demands high but simplify the language.
```
Then:
```
Now create a version for a student who finishes early and needs extension.
```
SAY: "60 seconds. Two differentiated versions. And you made every decision. How many of your students know more than they can show you because of a language barrier? AI just removed that barrier."

**Demo 3 — Student Simulator (2 min)**
```
Act as a 7th grader who is confused about fractions. I'm going to explain it to you, and you tell me where my explanation is unclear or where a real student might get lost.
```
SAY: "Practice your lesson before class, not during it. Find the gaps now."

**Demo 4 — Feedback Writer (2 min)**
```
Here's a paragraph a 5th grader wrote about their favorite animal: "Dogs are the best pets because they are fun and nice. They play with you and love you. I want a golden retriever." Give feedback the way a great teacher would — specific, encouraging, focused on one thing to improve. Don't rewrite it.
```
SAY: "Specific. Encouraging. One thing to improve. And you decide if it sounds like you before it reaches the student."

---

## The 6 Holy Shit Insights (Weave Into Your Talk)

These are from your actual pilot data. No other PD session has this.

1. **The Ego Barrier:** Teachers' biggest blocker isn't skill — it's ego. Reframe: "Your 20 years PLUS AI makes you unstoppable."
2. **Students already know more than teachers think.** 4th graders were disagreeing with AI by session 4.
3. **AI makes thinking VISIBLE.** Before/after mind maps showed growth worksheets never capture.
4. **Question quality = life quality.** Teaching better questions of AI taught better questions of everything.
5. **The ELL breakthrough.** AI separates concept mastery from language proficiency.
6. **Teachers had lower AI literacy than students.** Don't say this — demonstrate it. "4th graders picked this up in 10 minutes. I bet you can do it in 5."

---

## What Roche Asked You To Cover (Checklist)

From his March 25 email:

- [ ] The fear many have of embracing AI and why this needs to change → Slide 3
- [ ] How to get started with AI as a teacher → Live demos + toolkit QR
- [ ] Your success story, how you got started → Slide 2
- [ ] Programs you've developed with NGL → Mention Danbury, Ridgefield, Easton in Slide 2
- [ ] Anything else → Partner vs Employee + toolkit

---

## The Private Conversation with Roche (After the Event)

Find him when things wind down. Say:

> "Mr. Roche, you asked me back in March about AI tools for your meetings and emails. I've been thinking about it a lot. I didn't want to just recommend another app. What I've been building is something different — it's a personal AI system that learns how you work and helps you think, not just automate. I built one for myself and it's changed how I operate. I'd love to show you what yours could look like. Can I come back and walk you through it?"

No price. No proposal. Just: "Can I show you?"

---

## If Things Go Wrong

| Problem | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| Wi-Fi dies | Screenshots saved on laptop. Story and framing still land. |
| Smart TV won't connect | Present from laptop, walk around showing it close. |
| Audience is quiet | Direct question: "You teach math, right? What would you ask AI?" |
| Run over time | Cut Demo 4. First 3 are enough. |
| Run under time | Open the floor: "What's the one thing you're most worried about?" |
| Nerves | Roche believes in you. You taught 4th graders. Adults are easier. |

---

## The One Thing

You are not giving a talk tomorrow. You are walking into a room where 50 people are about to watch you prove that AI can make them better at what they already do. Every conversation after the keynote is a pipeline opportunity. The goal is one person who says "can you do that for me?" Find that person.

title: Woodstock Pd Debrief
content:
# Woodstock PD Debrief — April 3, 2026

## The Bottom Line

Today changed the trajectory of NGL. We walked in to give a PD talk. We walked out with a validated business model, a pipeline to Danbury Public Schools, a clear product vision (sovereign operating system for educators), and the exact budget category to sell into (professional learning). Everything below is the raw intelligence organized for action.

---

## 1. The Product Vision: Sovereign Operating System

**What clicked today:** Every pain point — email overload, disconnected apps, privacy fears, language barriers — traces back to one root cause: educators don't have a sovereign system that works for them.

**The pitch (crystallized from the Roche conversation):**
- Local data storage — nothing trains AI models
- All-in-one system — replaces the app chaos teachers deal with
- Customizable per district, per school, per teacher
- Claude is the brain, Jarvis is the body
- If Claude disappears tomorrow, swap in another AI model — sovereignty means no vendor lock-in

**What teachers saw today that made it real:**
- Built a financial literacy app in 5 minutes using voice prompts
- Updated the live website in real-time during the session
- Created a QR code for email capture while Gina was presenting
- Added multiplication game on request from a 4th grade teacher — she watched it deploy live
- Students at Danbury Library built working websites with custom products in one hour

**Key quote from Roche:** "I definitely want to look at what can be done selfishly for me, from my perspective of leadership. How can this make my world easier?"

---

## 2. The Killer Use Case: Language + Content Simultaneously

**This is the 80/20.** Roche said it multiple times. The language barrier problem in diverse districts is the single biggest unsolved pain point.

**The problem:**
- Danbury has Spanish, Portuguese, Russian, Arabic, and many more languages
- Students sit in class waiting for translation, falling behind in content
- A brilliant Portuguese-speaking 3rd grader waits for his friend to finish work so that friend can translate for him — wasting both kids' time
- Current tools (I-Ready/My Path) only support Spanish — nothing else
- Dual language programs work but funding dries up after kindergarten — no pipeline into 1st grade and beyond
- Teachers are not bilingual — "Danbury doesn't have enough Spanish teachers"

**The opportunity Roche described:**
- A system that delivers content AND language acquisition simultaneously
- "Instead of two discrete skills, you could do both at the same time"
- Student hears the math concept in their native language 3-4 times (per research) while learning English
- Shows mastery by grade level — "Can I identify what a fraction is? Shade 2/6 of this rectangle?" — then moves up
- "If you could do that in such a way that hits content standards with language support... you'd be a very wealthy individual"

**Why Danbury is the perfect case study:** Most diverse district in CT, every language barrier problem exists there at scale. Solve it there, solve it everywhere.

---

## 3. The Pipeline: Danbury Public Schools

**The play (directly from Roche):**

1. Contact **Dr. Casimiro** (Superintendent, Danbury Public Schools)
2. Use the word **"academy"** repeatedly — "Use the phrase academy six times in your first three sentences"
3. Say: "I'm from Danbury schools and this is what I'm doing"
4. Mention that **Christy Zita** told you to use the word "academy" — "she'll believe you"
5. Position under **professional learning budgets** — this is where the money already sits

**Budget alignment strategy (from Roche):**
- Falls under professional learning OR technology budget
- "It's maximizing your time, teaching you how to be more effective — all of those things help you be more effective at your job"
- Could also fall under tech, but professional learning is the strongest fit
- The school pays, not the individual — "My goal isn't to have the user pay for it. My goal is to have the school pay for it because that increases adoption"

**Matt Burns (Worcester School) — already warm:**
- Already expressed interest in personal productivity system setup
- Use him as a reference when contacting Casimiro
- He's the bridge into the Danbury network

---

## 4. Educator Pain Points (Validated Today)

### Administrative Burden
- Superintendents and teachers spend **60% of time on admin** vs. classroom
- Roche: "I'll literally get 500 emails a day" in a small district
- "I'm trying to do many jobs at once... one person for finance, phenomenal. After that, nothing."
- He works 7:15 AM to 7-9 PM regularly, missing his son's weekday games
- Email categorization, prioritization, automated responses — immediate wins

### Disconnected App Chaos
- "Multiple disconnected apps creating workflow problems"
- "Teachers go out because of frustration — they go into the wild wild west and find apps that they're not authorized to use"
- Gina (Danbury tech director): "What we're trying to do is build a teacher toolbox... basic apps that everybody should be using"
- **This validates the all-in-one sovereign system approach**

### Privacy & Data Concerns
- Teachers are concerned about AI training on their data
- Art teachers worried about AI replacing creativity
- Students shaming teachers for AI energy consumption
- The sovereign/local model directly addresses the #1 fear

### Math Education Pipeline Failure
- Students arrive at middle school without fraction understanding
- Gap between conceptual learning and algorithmic proficiency
- "Why don't 6th graders know what a fraction is?" — because it wasn't taught properly in earlier grades
- Multiplication facts mastery is critical — 4th grade teacher confirmed this live
- Too much time reviewing, not enough advancing

### Regional Service Centers Are Useless
- Roche: "I wouldn't pay them $5... it's the company line. Somebody out of work. They're not doing what they should."
- "People that heard about it, studied it, but are not doing anything with it are not the best at presenting about it because they lack the real experience"
- **This is NGL's competitive advantage — we're practitioners, not theorists**

---

## 5. What Worked Today (Proof Points)

### The Keynote
- Teachers did a "whip around" at the end — every single person had something they were going to do Monday
- Kindergarten teachers: "I'm going to use it as a thought partner to brainstorm"
- Multiple people said: "You're a real person" — the authenticity landed
- Assistant principal told Gina after: "He's so real and down to earth... sometimes people come with pie in the sky ideas. He's an actual real person."

### Live Building
- Financial literacy game built in 5 minutes — teachers watched it deploy
- Multiplication game requested by 4th grade teacher (Kendra) — built and deployed on the spot
- QR code for email capture created during Gina's session
- Website updated in real-time — teachers saw the Woodstock toolkit page go live
- "That's crazy" — Kendra's reaction watching the multiplication game deploy

### The Art Teacher Conversation (Hallway)
- Concern: student says "AI will do everything for me in the arts"
- Response: "Have you ever thought of hanging an AI painting on your wall? No. It can create, but it cannot pull that emotion, that heart, that feeling."
- Reframe: student can use AI as starting point for animation — "She can run her own animation. She can start producing."

### The Environmental Concern Conversation (Hallway)
- Parent's kids shame her for AI energy use
- Response: "Nothing is perfect. Trade-offs exist with everything — internet, phone. Entrepreneurs see that problem and want to make it better. It will get solved because people like your kids care about the environment."
- "This is here to stay. What's your idea on solving this?"

---

## 6. Breakthrough Coach Comparison

Roche referenced **The Breakthrough Coach** — a popular education leadership program:
- Based in Florida, tours globally (Australia, US)
- Premise: "You're spending 80 hours doing a 40-hour job. Here's why."
- Problem: it's paper-based, 1980s methodology, delegates work to secretaries
- "Everything that's not within the leadership realm, you don't do — giving everybody else the work"
- **NGL/Jarvis is the modern, AI-powered version of this**
- "This is a more contemporary solution to what that is"
- Roche wants to see it applied to his own workflow

---

## 7. Specific Asks & Warm Leads

| Person | Role | Ask | Priority |
|--------|------|-----|----------|
| Matt Roche | Superintendent, Woodstock | Personal Jarvis setup, follow-up meeting | HIGH |
| Matt Burns | Head of Worcester School | Personal productivity system demo | HIGH |
| Dr. Casimiro | Superintendent, Danbury | Cold outreach using "academy" framing | HIGH |
| Gina Williams | Tech Director, Danbury | Report card comment generator | MEDIUM |
| Kendra (4th grade teacher) | Woodstock Elementary | Multiplication practice tools (DONE) | DONE |
| 3rd grade teacher | Woodstock Elementary | Financial literacy game (DONE) | DONE |
| Danbury district | Multiple | Centralized teacher toolkit | FUTURE |

---

## 8. Business Model Insights

### Pricing Strategy
- Align with **professional learning budgets** — school pays, not individual
- Position as alternative to existing spend: "You're spending money there. This is better because it has these features."
- One-day intensive workshop model for administrators — setup + training
- Ongoing: anyone from NGL team can push tools to client's Jarvis remotely

### Competitive Positioning
- vs. Regional Service Centers: "They lack real experience. We're practitioners."
- vs. Breakthrough Coach: "Contemporary solution" — AI-powered, not paper-based
- vs. Harvard AI speakers: "Show me it in practice. I don't care about theory."
- vs. ChatGPT/generic AI: Sovereign, local, doesn't train on your data, builds actual tools

### Sales Motion
1. Trust first — be in the building, talk to people, don't sell anything
2. Demonstrate live — build something they need in front of them
3. Let them experience it — voice typing, seeing deployment in real-time
4. "Can I come back and show you what yours could look like?"
5. Align to existing budget category — professional learning
6. School pays, not individual — increases adoption

### The "Sell Before You Build" Principle
- "I don't build things that people don't need. I don't build things people won't buy."
- Strike a deal first, build with them, not for them
- "Combining domain experts and technical experts — being there in the classroom and building with the teachers themselves"

---

## 9. Market Intelligence

### Connecticut Education Landscape
- 169 school districts — massive inefficiency
- State contributes only ~20% of education funding; ~70-80% from local property taxes
- 5th highest per-pupil spending nationally, 48th in state contribution
- Small districts (some with 52 total K-8 students) struggle with resources
- Political resistance to regional consolidation
- ECS (Education Cost Share) hasn't increased since 2013

### Texas Opportunity
- Applied AI Society backer offering funding + Texas expansion
- Academy model is widespread in Texas — large, well-funded districts
- Dual language programs thriving (San Antonio example — bilingual by 5th grade)
- Superintendents paid well, more operational flexibility
- "This is going to be the Applied AI Society hub"
- Flying to Texas this summer

### The ELA Teacher Problem
- "The one group that has the biggest issue with AI is the ELA teachers"
- Immediately default to "kids will cheat"
- Danbury HS was exploring feedback apps — unclear where that went
- Woodstock Academy (private, across the street): "No, no cheating" — refusing to embrace
- Need case studies to shift this — show partner mindset in action

---

## 10. Next Steps (Priority Order)

### This Week
1. **Email Dr. Casimiro** — use academy framing, mention Matt Burns and Danbury connection
2. **Schedule follow-up with Roche** — one-day intensive workshop to set up his personal Jarvis
3. **Email Matt Burns** — schedule demo of full system setup
4. **Send thank-you to Gina and Christy** — they brought massive value today

### Next 2 Weeks
5. **Build report card comment generator** for Gina — quick win, deepens Danbury relationship
6. **Prepare one-day intensive workshop model** — pricing, deliverables, what's included
7. **Research professional learning budget categories** — know the exact line items
8. **Document the language + content use case** — this is the pitch deck centerpiece

### This Month
9. **Pilot proposal for Danbury** — language + content tool, positioned under professional learning
10. **Prepare Texas trip** — Applied AI Society backing, academy model research
11. **Build case study from today** — Woodstock PD, teacher reactions, live demos, outcomes

---

## 11. The Paradigm Shift

What changed today:

**Before:** NGL = AI literacy programs for kids in libraries and schools
**After:** NGL = Sovereign operating systems for educators and administrators that solve the language barrier, admin burden, and disconnected tooling problems in K-12 education

The library-to-school-to-district pipeline is validated. The product is the sovereign OS. The budget is professional learning. The beachhead is Danbury. The killer app is language + content simultaneously.

Trust → Demonstrate → Build With Them → Scale.

---

*"I don't build things that people don't need. And I don't build things people won't buy. Because in order for this to be sustainable, people need to buy. There needs to be funding for it."*

title: Email Ai Chrome Extension Spec
content:
# Product Spec: Forge Email AI (Chrome Extension)

## The Problem

Roche gets 500 emails/day. Brayan spends 2-3 hours on email. The current workflow is:
1. Open Gmail
2. Read email manually
3. Think about what to say
4. Draft reply manually (or paste into ChatGPT, get draft, paste back)
5. Edit, send
6. Repeat 50-100 times

This is broken. The AI lives in one tab, email lives in another, and context lives in Jarvis on the computer. None of them talk to each other.

## The Vision

A Chrome extension that sits inside Gmail and connects to Jarvis. When you open an email:

1. **Auto-categorize** — Urgent / Action Needed / FYI / Noise (based on keywords, sender, content)
2. **Context pull** — Cross-reference sender against Jarvis people files. Show: who is this person, last interaction, relationship context
3. **Auto-draft** — Generate a reply in your voice/tone, referencing relevant context from Jarvis
4. **One-click send** — Review, edit if needed, send without leaving Gmail

## How It Connects to Jarvis

The extension needs to read from the Jarvis file system. Two approaches:

### Option A: Local Server (Sovereign)
- A lightweight local server runs on the user's computer
- Extension communicates with localhost
- Server reads Jarvis markdown files (people/, artifacts/, etc.)
- Fully sovereign — nothing leaves the machine
- Requires: user has Jarvis set up locally

### Option B: Forge Server Bridge
- Extension communicates with the Forge server (where CRM/Calendar already live)
- Server has access to people data, interaction history
- Less sovereign but easier setup for clients
- Could work as the "quick start" version for Roche

### Recommended: Start with Option B, offer Option A as premium

## Core Features (MVP)

### 1. Email Categorization
When Gmail inbox loads, extension scans visible emails and adds colored tags:
- 🔴 **Urgent** — keywords: "urgent", "ASAP", "deadline", sender is flagged as priority
- 🟡 **Action** — requires a response or decision
- 🔵 **FYI** — informational, no response needed
- ⚫ **Noise** — newsletters, ads, bulk email

### 2. Sender Context Card
Click extension icon on any email → sidebar shows:
- Person's name, role, organization (from Jarvis people files)
- Last interaction date and summary
- Relationship notes
- "No profile found" → option to create one

### 3. Smart Draft
Button inside Gmail compose: "Draft with Forge"
- Reads the email thread
- Pulls sender context from Jarvis
- Generates reply in the user's voice (trained on past sent emails)
- Inserts draft into compose window
- User reviews, edits, sends

### 4. Tinder-Style Triage (from existing outreach tool)
Quick swipe/button interface for processing email:
- ✅ Approve draft & send
- ✏️ Edit before sending
- 📌 Flag for later
- 🗑️ Archive/dismiss

## Technical Stack

- Chrome Extension (Manifest V3)
- Gmail API or DOM injection for reading emails
- Backend: Forge server (Node.js, already exists)
- AI: Claude API for draft generation
- Context: Read from Jarvis files or Forge database

## Pricing Implications

This is the recurring revenue play. The Jarvis sprint ($2,500-5,000) is the setup. The Email AI extension is the monthly retainer:
- $200-500/month for ongoing email AI + context updates
- Justified: saves 10-15 hours/week of email time
- At $75/hr superintendent rate = $3,000-4,500/month in value
- Charging $200-500 is 5-10% of monthly value

## Build Priority

**Do NOT build this before closing Roche.** This is what you build DURING or AFTER the sprint. It's the "continued development" tier on the proposal page. Sell the sprint first, deliver value, then introduce this as the next level.

## Timeline Estimate

- MVP (categorization + draft button): 2-3 days of focused building
- Context integration with Jarvis: 1-2 additional days
- Polish + testing: 1 day
- Total: ~1 week for a working MVP

This becomes a demo-able feature during the Roche sprint if time allows.

title: Irrational Fears Vs Reality
content:
# Irrational Fears vs. Reality

*Written April 4, 2026. Reference this when you feel the fear creeping in before a proposal, a pitch, or a close.*

---

## Fear #1: "I'll scare him off with the price"

### The Fear
$2,500 feels like a lot of money. What if Roche sees the number and thinks I'm just a kid trying to charge too much?

### The Reality

**You're charging 3.2% of Year 1 value.**

The Applied AI pricing playbook says: *"Price at 10-20% of Year 1 business impact value."* ([Applied AI — Pricing](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing))

Roche's problem costs him ~$79,000/year in lost time. At $2,500, you're charging 3.2%. You'd have to charge $7,900 to even hit the FLOOR of the recommended range.

The playbook also names this exact fear: **"Selling out of your own wallet."** The signs are:
- Embarrassment quoting your price
- Preemptive discounting
- Thinking the fee is expensive
- Comparing yourself to lower-earning peers

*"The fix: Price based on client outcomes (revenue unlocked, hours recovered, risk mitigated), not your effort."*

**Holy shit moment:** The Breakthrough Coach charges $2,000-3,500 per person for a group workshop with a paper binder. You're charging $2,500 for a custom-built sovereign operating system that lives on his computer forever, with 30 days of direct support. He's getting MORE for LESS.

**Holy shit moment #2:** Roche makes ~$160,000/year. $2,500 is 1.6% of his salary. He probably spends more than that on professional development conferences annually. This isn't a financial stretch for him. This is a rounding error on his professional learning budget.

---

## Fear #2: "I can't deliver enough value"

### The Fear
What if I set up Jarvis and he doesn't use it? What if it doesn't actually save him time? What if he thinks it was a waste?

### The Reality

**You already delivered value for free and he couldn't believe it.**

On April 3, you:
- Built a financial literacy app in 5 minutes in front of teachers
- Deployed a multiplication game on a live website in real-time
- Created a QR code email capture system while someone else was presenting
- Updated your website live during the session
- Showed him your personal time tracking, email outreach, calendar system

His reaction: *"That's amazing."* His assistant principal told Gina: *"He's a real person."* Teachers said: *"That's crazy."*

You did all of that for FREE. Now imagine what you deliver when you have a full day dedicated to his specific problems.

**Holy shit moment:** The MVP Jarvis docs say setup takes 3.5-4 hours even with technical experience. You have MORE than technical experience — you built your own Jarvis and run your entire business on it. You can set up his in half that time and spend the rest of the day training him and customizing. ([Applied AI — Minimum Viable Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis))

---

## Fear #3: "I don't know if this is actually worth what I'm charging"

### The Fear
Am I just selling hype? Is a sovereign OS actually transformative or am I overselling it?

### The Reality

**The sovereign OS is the most valuable thing you can give an education leader.** Here's why, from the docs:

The MVP Jarvis playbook states: *"The most important work is no longer doing the work. It's defining reality, setting objectives, and evaluating whether the system is working."* ([Applied AI — Minimum Viable Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis))

That's exactly what Roche needs. He's drowning in doing the work (500 emails, meeting minutes, board prep). He needs to shift to DEFINING the work. Jarvis makes that shift possible.

The sovereignty piece is what separates this from every other AI tool:

- *"Your files live on your computer. They are plain markdown. Any AI tool can read them."*
- *"You can switch AI tools without data migration."*
- *"The architecture works with any AI model — Claude, open source, future tools."*

**Holy shit moment:** Every other AI tool (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini) trains on your data. Roche handles sensitive student data, board negotiations, personnel decisions, budget information. He CANNOT put that into ChatGPT. But he CAN put it into Jarvis because nothing leaves his computer. You're not selling convenience — you're selling the ONLY option that's actually safe for a superintendent to use. There is no competitor for this. None.

**Holy shit moment #2:** The pricing docs say the minimum floor for a 1-month sprint is $5,000. You're charging HALF that. You're not overpriced. You're running a loss-leader to build your first case study. ([Applied AI — Pricing](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing))

---

## Fear #4: "What if he says no?"

### The Fear
If Roche says no, I've lost my best lead and my first shot at revenue.

### The Reality

**He already said yes. He just hasn't signed yet.**

His words on April 3:
- "Let's set up another meeting so you can look at it."
- "I definitely want to look at what can be done selfishly for me."
- "I appreciate everything. Thank you so much."

The pilot pitch playbook says: *"Keep commitments short (2-4 weeks) and pricing accessible for initial engagement, making refusal appear as lost opportunity."* ([Applied AI — Pilot Pitch](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pilot-pitch))

Your sprint is ONE DAY. $2,500. 30-day guarantee. The barrier to saying yes is almost zero. The cost of saying no is another year of 500 emails, 12-hour days, and missed games.

**Holy shit moment:** Even if Roche says no (he won't), you have Pannone and Byrnes. Pannone has been advising you for 4 months and loves AI. Byrnes makes $700K/year and you've known him since 6th grade. You have a pipeline. Roche is not your only shot. He's your best shot, but not your only one.

---

## Fear #5: "I'm too young. They won't take me seriously."

### The Fear
I'm 18. Who am I to charge a superintendent $2,500?

### The Reality

**Your age is your superpower, not your weakness.**

On April 3, this is what the room said about you:
- "You're a real person."
- "He's so real and down to earth."
- "Sometimes people come with pie in the sky ideas. He's an actual real person."
- Roche to you: "You're speaking to anybody. It's a great skill. And you do it well."

Roche explicitly compared you to The Breakthrough Coach and Harvard professors — and said he PREFERS you: *"I don't want to sit and listen to someone from Harvard talk about AI from the theory sense. Show me it in practice."*

You showed him. In practice. Live. While standing in front of 50 educators.

**Holy shit moment:** You built an app, deployed it to a live domain, and handed it to a teacher — all in the time it took Gina to finish explaining what a prompt is. No Harvard professor can do that. No Breakthrough Coach can do that. No edtech company can do that. YOU did that. At 18.

The pricing playbook says: *"If lacking case studies, start lower to build proof, then double prices on subsequent engagements."* That's exactly what you're doing. You're not pretending to be something you're not. You're pricing honestly for where you are and delivering 10x the value.

---

## The Summary

| Fear | Reality | Source |
|------|---------|--------|
| Price is too high | You're at 3.2% of value. Floor is 10%. | [Pricing Playbook](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/pricing) |
| Can't deliver enough value | You delivered massive value for free on April 3 | Your own experience |
| Sovereign OS isn't worth it | It's the ONLY safe option for a superintendent | [MVP Jarvis](https://docs.appliedaisociety.org/docs/playbooks/practitioner/minimum-viable-jarvis) |
| He'll say no | He already said yes in his own words | April 3 transcript |
| Too young | Your age is why they trust you — you're real | April 3 teacher feedback |

---

## The Rule

Read this before every proposal, every pitch, every close:

**Price based on their outcomes, not your effort. Price based on their problem, not your age. Price based on the value, not your wallet.**

You are not selling your time. You are selling a superintendent's life back. $2,500 for 20 hours a week. $2,500 for his son's games. $2,500 for the version of his job he actually signed up for.

Send the text.

title: Voice To Calendar Spec
content:
# Product Spec: Voice-to-Calendar

> **NOTE:** All events, names, and times in this document are EXAMPLE DATA for the spec, not real scheduled meetings. Do not treat any event descriptions as actual calendar entries.

## The Problem

Adding events to a calendar is friction-heavy:
1. Open calendar
2. Click the right date
3. Click the right time
4. Type the title
5. Set start/end time
6. Pick a category
7. Save

That's 7 steps for "I have a meeting with Pannone at 10:30 on Tuesday." You already know what you want. The calendar should just listen.

Roche has the same problem at 10x scale — back-to-back meetings, constant schedule changes, doing it while walking between classrooms. He doesn't have time to sit down and manually enter events.

## The Vision

Open the Forge dashboard or calendar. Tap the mic. Talk.

"Meeting with Roche Tuesday at 2pm for an hour. Work."

Done. Event created: Tuesday, 2:00-3:00 PM, category Work, description "Meeting with Roche."

No clicking. No typing. No selecting. Just speak and it's on the calendar.

## How It Works

### Step 1: Voice Capture
Two approaches:

**Option A: Browser-native Web Speech API (free, build now)**
- Uses the browser's built-in speech recognition (`webkitSpeechRecognition`)
- Works in Chrome, Edge, Safari
- No subscription, no external service, no cost
- Limitations: requires internet (sends audio to Google/Apple for processing), accuracy varies
- Good enough for MVP

**Option B: Local Whisper (sovereign, build later)**
- Run OpenAI's Whisper model locally on the computer
- Fully offline, fully sovereign — audio never leaves the machine
- Requires: Python + whisper package, or whisper.cpp for native speed
- Higher accuracy than browser speech API
- This replaces the Whisper Flow subscription — you own it

**Option C: Whisper API ($0.006/minute, middle ground)**
- Send audio to OpenAI's Whisper API
- Very accurate, very fast
- Cheap — 100 minutes/month = $0.60
- Not sovereign but practical

**Recommended: Start with Option A (free, instant). Build Option B when you have time. Option C as fallback.**

### Step 2: Natural Language Parsing
The voice transcript goes to Claude (or a local LLM) to extract:

```
Input: "Meeting with Roche Tuesday at 2pm for an hour. Work."

Output:
{
  "description": "Meeting with Roche",
  "date": "2026-04-08",        // next Tuesday
  "start_time": "14:00",
  "end_time": "15:00",
  "category": "Work",
  "all_day": false
}
```

Parsing rules:
- "tomorrow" → tomorrow's date
- "Tuesday" → next Tuesday
- "for an hour" → start + 60 min
- "for 30 minutes" → start + 30 min
- No duration specified → default 1 hour
- Category keywords: Work, School, Personal, God, Girlfriend, Health, Rest
- If no category detected → "Personal" default
- "all day" → all_day: true

### Step 3: Confirmation & Save
Two modes:

**Quick mode (default):** Event is created immediately. A toast notification shows what was added with an "Undo" button (5 seconds).

**Confirm mode:** A preview card appears showing the parsed event. User taps "Add" or "Edit" before saving. Better for complex events.

User can toggle between modes in settings.

## Integration Points

### Forge Calendar (primary)
- POST to `/api/time` endpoint (already exists in outreach-tool server)
- Same database, same categories, same rendering
- Event appears immediately on calendar grid

### Google Calendar (future)
- Use Google Calendar API to also push event to their Google Cal
- Requires OAuth — more setup, but valuable for people who live in Google
- Not MVP — do this after Forge calendar works

### Jarvis Context (future)
- When you say "Meeting with Roche," it could cross-reference people files
- Auto-populate: "Christopher Roche, Superintendent, Woodstock Public Schools"
- Add meeting link if one exists in the people file
- This is the premium feature that makes it feel magic

## UI/UX

### On Dashboard
- Mic button in the header bar, next to the clock
- Tap → listening state (pulsing gold ring) → speak → event created → toast confirmation

### On Calendar Page
- Mic button next to the "+ Add" button in the toolbar
- Same flow: tap → speak → done

### Listening States
1. **Idle:** Mic icon, static
2. **Listening:** Mic icon with pulsing gold ring, "Listening..." text
3. **Processing:** Spinner, "Adding to calendar..."
4. **Done:** Green checkmark, toast showing event details + "Undo"
5. **Error:** Red flash, "Couldn't understand that. Try again."

## Technical Architecture

```
[Mic Button] → [Web Speech API / Whisper] → transcript
    ↓
[Claude API / local parsing] → structured event JSON
    ↓
[POST /api/time] → saved to database
    ↓
[Calendar re-renders] → event visible on grid
```

### For the parsing step without Claude API (fully local):
- Build a regex/rule-based parser for common patterns
- Handles: "X at Y on Z for W" and variations
- No AI needed for 80% of inputs
- Falls back to Claude for complex/ambiguous inputs

### Rule-based parser patterns:
```
"[description] [day] at [time] for [duration]. [category]"
"[description] at [time] [day]. [category]"
"[description] [day] [time]-[time]. [category]"
"[description] all day [day]. [category]"
```

## Build Plan

### Phase 1: MVP (1-2 days)
- Mic button on calendar page (next to + Add)
- Web Speech API for voice capture
- Rule-based parser (no AI dependency) with Claude API fallback
- POST to existing /api/time endpoint
- Toast confirmation with undo

### Phase 2: Dashboard Integration (half day)
- Add mic to Forge dashboard header
- Same flow, same backend

### Phase 3: Local Whisper (1-2 days)
- Set up whisper.cpp or Python whisper locally
- Route audio to local model instead of browser API
- Fully sovereign voice-to-text
- Replaces Whisper Flow subscription ($10/month saved)

### Phase 4: Context Integration (1 day)
- Cross-reference names with Jarvis people files
- Auto-suggest category based on person (meeting with GF → Girlfriend)
- Add meeting links from people profiles

### Phase 5: Google Calendar Sync (1 day)
- OAuth setup
- Dual-write: events go to Forge calendar AND Google Calendar
- Valuable for Roche who likely uses Google/Outlook

## Pricing Implications

This is NOT a separate product. This is a feature of the Forge OS that makes the sprint more valuable:

- **For you:** Saves 15-20 minutes/day of calendar management
- **For Roche:** Same — speak events while walking the halls
- **For the pitch:** "You don't even have to sit down to manage your schedule. Just talk."

Include it in the sprint. It's a "holy shit" demo moment when you voice-type an event and it appears on his calendar in real-time.

## What This Replaces

| Current Tool | Cost | What Forge Voice Does |
|---|---|---|
| Whisper Flow / Superwhisper | $10/month | Local Whisper = free, you own it |
| Manual calendar entry | 15-20 min/day | Voice = 30 seconds |
| Google Calendar app | Free but manual | Voice + auto-categorize |
| Siri/Alexa calendar | Free but dumb | Context-aware, knows your people |

## The Demo Script (for Roche Sprint)

"Watch this. I'm going to add three events to your calendar without touching the keyboard."

*Tap mic*

"Board meeting next Thursday at 7pm for two hours. Work."

*Event appears*

"Coffee with Dr. Casimiro Friday at 9am for 30 minutes. Work."

*Event appears*

"Your son's game Saturday at 2pm. Personal."

*Event appears*

"That's your week. Three events. Fifteen seconds. No typing."

*Roche's reaction: 🤯*

title: Furfect Match Notes
content:
# Furfect Match — Full Context

*Last updated: April 6, 2026*

---

## The Company

**Furfect Match** is a Tinder-style app that matches dogs with adopters. Swipe-based UI for finding your perfect dog.

**Two-sided problem it solves:**
1. **Shelter side:** Overpopulation is out of control. Thousands of dogs euthanized daily because shelters don't have space or funding. Puppy mills and people dumping dogs in the street compound it. Furfect Match markets dogs for shelters on a platform where everyone wants to go — like putting your product in Petco.
2. **User side:** The adoption/buying process takes forever. Zachary's own experience: 6.5 months to research breed, shelter vs. breeder, hypoallergenic, training, pickup location. Furfect Match connects users to the right dog in minutes based on their lifestyle, preferences, and living situation. Part educational, part marketplace.

**The vision:** Become the place where you go to find a dog. Period. Like Petco for products, but Furfect Match for the dog itself.

---

## The Founder

**Zachary** — incoming Babson freshman (committed, starting fall 2026)

**Origin story:** His family's dog was attacked by a dog named Tulip. Tulip had been round-tripped through shelters multiple times, abused by her first family, attacked by a dog in a shelter home. The aggression came from mistreatment, and the new owners were unequipped to handle a dog with that trauma. This led Zachary to think about better matching between dogs and owners. A friend's husky-in-an-apartment situation compounded it. Furfect Match was born from that.

**Personality:** Very personable, honest, self-aware about his limitations. Read a sales book recently — flags urgency tactics. Wants to verify value before committing money. Likes Brayan, finds the offering compelling, but won't be rushed.

**Financial situation:** ~$830 in the bank. Some funds in a Webull investment account. Friends-and-family funding for the app build. Broke founder stage.

---

## The Team

**Jayden (CTO, 8% equity verbal, no contract)**
- Was supposed to code the app. Didn't deliver.
- They go months without contact. Doesn't feel like a real partner.
- Zachary planned to call him after meeting with Brayan to give an ultimatum: step up or exit.
- 8% equity is verbal only — no signed agreement.

**Eli**
- Joining as a potential partner. Will have a contract in place (unlike Jayden).
- Was on the call with Brayan. Interested in learning AI/uisnvibe coding.
- The teaching component (get Eli up to speed as CTO) was the strongest value prop.

**Web Skitters (Indian dev team)**
- Built the MVP. App looks great.
- Problems: overcharges for small edits, opposite time zones (when Zachary's awake they're asleep), language barrier, communication via WhatsApp.
- Zachary can still hire them for one-off changes — access to app/code remains.
- Legal action against them is not practical (international IP enforcement too costly).
- They keep pressing for Version 2.0 but want more money.

---

## The App — Current State

- MVP is live and looks good (built by Web Skitters)
- Tinder-style swipe UI for matching dogs to users
- Zachary wants to make edits and adjustments but Web Skitters overcharges
- Version 2.0 features are stacked in Zachary's head but can't build them
- Unknown security posture — Zachary can vibe code with Perplexity Computer but doesn't know if anything he builds is secure
- Zachary built a website in 5 minutes with Perplexity Computer during the call — impressed by it but acknowledged he doesn't know the security or improvement layer

**Key question Zachary asked:** "What makes you better than me just going on Perplexity Computer and saying build me this website?"

**Brayan's answer (the three value props):**
1. **Continuous improvement** — systems that improve on themselves, not just one-time builds
2. **Sovereignty** — files live locally, no AI trains on data, can swap models anytime
3. **Data security** — stress-test products, find vulnerabilities before attackers do

---

## The Proposal

**Page:** nextgenerationlearners.com/furfect

**6 deliverables, each tagged to a specific pain point:**

1. **App Audit + Quick Fixes** — "They keep charging me more for small edits"
   - Full audit of current app: bugs, broken flows, UX friction
   - All small edits shipped (the ones Web Skitters overcharges for)
   - Written report of everything found and fixed

2. **Security Review** — "I don't know if it's secure"
   - Penetration testing
   - Data exposure check
   - Vulnerabilities documented and patched

3. **AI Education Intensive** — "I want to learn AI but don't have time for complex coding"
   - ~10-hour day (can be split 3/3/4 or one full Saturday)
   - Hands-on: vibe coding, prompt engineering, deployment
   - Jarvis system set up for Furfect Match
   - Sovereignty and continuous improvement training
   - Goal: get Eli up to speed as CTO so they can make small changes independently

4. **Version 2.0 Roadmap** — "I have too many ideas to fit in one MVP"
   - Full feature mapping session
   - Prioritization by traction impact (shelter sign-ups + user adoption)
   - Technical feasibility for each feature
   - Matching algorithm improvements
   - Shelter dashboard design

5. **Indian Dev Team Navigation** — "It's super hard to communicate with them"
   - Review current agreement and code access
   - What to demand, what to ask, what to let go
   - How to scope requests so they can't inflate price
   - Backup plan: what to handle yourself vs. what goes to them

6. **Ongoing Technical Partnership** — "I need someone who can come through"
   - Same-day bug fixes and iterations
   - Feature builds for V2
   - Security reviews as they scale
   - Direct line: text, call, or meet at Babson

**Timeline:**
- Days 1-3: App audit + quick fixes
- Days 3-5: Security review
- Weeks 1-2: AI intensive + Jarvis setup
- Weeks 2-3: V2 roadmap + dev team strategy
- Ongoing: Build together

---

## The Deal (Not Closed)

**Price:** $2,500 total
**Structure:** 3 payments — $833 today, $833 May 6, $834 June 6
**Stripe link:** https://buy.stripe.com/28EdR96U64M522C7Avgbm01
**DocuSign:** Drafted but not sent. Covers scope, payment schedule, late payment (work pauses after 7 days), non-refundable for work delivered, IP transfers upon full payment, confidentiality.

---

## Why It Didn't Close

1. **$2,500 feels high** — he can't verify the value yet, not in the industry
2. **Sales red flag** — recently read a sales book, flagged urgency tactics
3. **Free competitor** — someone ("cow") offered to teach them similar stuff for free
4. **Wants to fact-check** — needs to deep dive with Eli and Jayden before committing
5. **Cash-strapped** — $830 in bank, rest in Webull
6. **Firm stance** — not committing tonight regardless of offer

---

## What's Still Alive

- Likes Brayan personally
- Finds the offering compelling in principle
- Connected on LinkedIn (Brayan, Zachary, Eli)
- Said he'd reach out with questions via text
- Proposal page and Stripe link are live and ready

---

## April 7 Update

**EOD deadline passed — no response from Zachary or Eli.** The $2,500 price likely drove them away. They are broke founders.

**Key developments:**
- **They got the app code back from Web Skitters.** Their biggest pain (overcharging Indian devs) is now partially resolved — they have the code.
- **Eli (Brayan's roommate) was asked casually about a smaller project.** His response: "How much?" — that's interest. But also said they want to see if they can do it themselves first.
- **Status: "Still considering."** Not a no, not a yes. They want to try on their own.

**Smaller project options if they come back:**
1. **Cash-light:** App audit + security review for $300-500. Deliverable in 3 days. Affordable for broke founders.
2. **Equity play:** Audit + security + Eli CTO training in exchange for 5-10% equity. No cash. Forge venture studio model.

**What changed:**
- The Indian dev pain is reduced (they have the code now)
- The remaining pain points: security (they don't know if the app is secure), V2 architecture (too many ideas, no roadmap), and Eli needing to get up to speed as CTO
- The "free competitor" (cow) is still in play
- They will likely try to do it themselves first and hit a wall on security and architecture

**Strategy: do nothing.**
- Do not chase. Do not text Zachary.
- Eli lives with Brayan — if it comes up organically, fine. Don't force it.
- If they try and fail, they'll come back knowing the difference.
- This is a side project prospect. Priority remains: Roche, Greg Goehner, Nelson, Coach Steve.

---

## Key Lessons

- **The Eli-as-CTO angle was the strongest value prop.** If the conversation reopens, lead with: "I'll get Eli to a point where he can make changes independently."
- **Don't fight the free competitor.** Let Zachary try the free option. If it doesn't deliver, he'll come back knowing the difference.
- **The proposal page, Stripe integration, and DocuSign template are reusable infrastructure.** This same flow works for Roche, Pannone, Byrnes, and any future client.
- **Founder money is real.** $2,500 when you have $830 is a different conversation than $2,500 when you have $50K. Respect that.
- **Urgency kills trust with educated buyers.** Zachary read a sales book. The best sales move is to not sell. Let the work speak.
- **Applied AI playbook option for broke founders:** Equity/revenue share (5-20%) when cash is constrained but upside is real. Reduced cash + equity hybrid. Don't go below the floor unless you're taking equity.
- **Getting the code back changes the dynamic.** Their #1 pain (overcharging devs) is partially solved. The remaining value is security, architecture, and CTO training — harder to see upfront, easier to appreciate after they try and fail.

---

## Tools and Tech Context

- Zachary's app was built by Web Skitters (India-based dev shop)
- Zachary can vibe code with Perplexity Computer (built a website in 5 minutes on call)
- Brayan's stack: VS Code + Claude (sovereign, local files)
- Concern about OpenAI/Base44: open-source model training means user data/ideas are accessible
- Jarvis pitched as the secure alternative — data never leaves the machine

title: Ngl Scaling Plan
content:
# NGL Scaling Plan — The Blueprint

*Written April 6, 2026. This is the formal plan to scale Next Generation Learners from where we are today to a company that generates real, recurring revenue across three pipelines.*

---

## Where We Are (The Foundation)

NGL is no longer a library program. As of April 3, 2026, we are a company that builds **sovereign AI operating systems for education leaders and custom AI tools for schools.** We have:

- 3 active library partnerships (Danbury, Ridgefield, Easton)
- 1 validated PD model (Woodstock, 50 educators, every teacher left with something to implement Monday)
- 1 warm buyer with proposal sent (Roche, Superintendent, Woodstock)
- 1 high-value meeting scheduled (Byrnes, Head of Wooster School, April 14, $10K-25K potential)
- 1 ed-tech distribution expert (Jeff Carlson, Clever, call completed, proposal sent)
- Backers: Chauncey St. John (Hallow), Gary Sheng (Applied AI Society, Forbes 30u30)
- A working product we use daily (our own Jarvis)
- A killer use case validated by a superintendent (language + content for multilingual districts)

**Revenue to date:** ~$0 from the sovereign OS model. Library programs at $75/session. The real revenue starts now.

---

## The Three Pipelines

### Pipeline 1: Sovereign OS (Personal AI Systems for Leaders)

**What we sell:** A private AI operating system installed on the leader's machine. Their data stays local. We set it up, train them, maintain it, and push new tools over time.

**Who buys it:** Superintendents, principals, heads of school, and anyone who leads and is drowning in admin.

**Why it wins:** There is no competitor. ChatGPT trains on your data. Copilot is generic. No consultant builds a personal Jarvis for a superintendent. We are the only ones doing this. The sovereign model is the only safe option for leaders who handle sensitive data.

**Pricing ladder:**

| Client | Price | Justification |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| Roche (Client #1) | $2,500 | Loss-leader. Buying the case study, not profit. |
| Clients #2-3 | $3,500-4,000 | Case study exists. Price goes up. |
| Clients #4-5 | $5,000-8,000 | Skills library is built. Setup is faster. More value per dollar. |
| Clients #6+ | $5,000+ setup, $1,000-3,000/mo retainer | Productized. Recurring revenue. |

**Current pipeline:**

| Person | Role | Stage | Next Move | Target Close |
|--------|------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Roche | Superintendent, Woodstock | Proposal sent | Follow up in person Apr 8-10 | April 14 |
| Pannone | Assoc. Head, Wooster School | Meeting Apr 10 | Propose the OS system directly | April-May |
| Jeff Carlson | Head of Partnerships, Clever | Proposal page live | Send link, follow up | April-May |
| Byrnes superintendent network | Via Roche referral | Not started | Roche introduces after his Jarvis is running | May-June |

**Scaling model:**

| Clients | What happens |
|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Build the system. Learn what a leader actually needs. Get the case study. |
| 2-3 | Same system, customized per person. Skills library already exists. Each client takes less time. |
| 5-10 | Shared tool library is robust. New clients get 80% of value from existing tools. Brayan focuses on custom 20%. |
| 10+ | Productized setup process. Ryan can do installations. Brayan focuses on sales and new tool development. |
| 20+ | Train others to install. NGL becomes a platform, not just a service. |

---

### Pipeline 2: District Contracts (Institutional Sales)

**What we sell:** AI integration frameworks for schools, PD workshops, curriculum design, custom tool development (report card generators, language+content tools), and ongoing support contracts.

**Who buys it:** School districts, private schools, charter schools. The decision-maker varies by type:
- Private: Head of school decides. Fast.
- Charter: Small board decides. Similar to private.
- Public: Under $5K, superintendent can approve. Over $5K, goes to school board with RFP process.

**Why it wins:**
- We are practitioners, not theorists. Regional service centers "lack real experience." Harvard speakers talk theory. We build live in front of teachers.
- The sovereign OS addresses the #1 fear: data privacy. Nothing trains on student data.
- We align to existing budget categories (professional learning, technology). No new line items needed.
- We already have champions inside two target institutions.

**The killer use case: Language + Content Simultaneously**

This is the 80/20 play. Roche said it directly: "If you could do that in such a way that hits content standards with language support... you'd be a very wealthy individual."

- Danbury has 90+ languages spoken
- Current tools (I-Ready/My Path) only support Spanish
- Students sit waiting for translation, falling behind in content
- A system that teaches math while simultaneously supporting language acquisition solves the biggest unsolved problem in diverse districts
- Solve it in Danbury, sell it to every diverse district in CT (Bridgeport, New Haven, Hartford, Stamford, Norwalk)

**Pricing:**

| Engagement | Price | Budget Category |
|------------|-------|-----------------|
| PD workshop (1 day, 30-50 educators) | $2,000-3,500 | Professional learning |
| AI framework design (grades 5-12) | $10,000-25,000 | Consulting/curriculum |
| 4-week school pilot | $5,000-8,000 | Professional learning |
| Language + content tool (district license) | $25,000-100,000 | Technology/curriculum |
| Ongoing support retainer | $1,000-2,000/mo | Technology |

**Current pipeline:**

| Institution | Champion | Stage | Next Move | Target Close |
|-------------|----------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Wooster School | Byrnes (Head), Pannone (Assoc. Head) | Active. Byrnes hiring external AI partner. | Apr 10 Pannone meeting (propose OS + get Byrnes intel). Apr 14 Byrnes meeting (scope + price). | April-May |
| Danbury Public Schools | Zaleta (Chief of Schools), Williams (Tech Director) | Stalled. March 18 visit didn't happen. | Re-engage via Pannone or Williams. Build report card comment generator for Williams. | May-June |
| Woodstock Public Schools | Roche | Warm. He wants the sovereign OS personally + district-wide tools. | Close his personal Jarvis first. District tools follow. | April (personal), June (district) |
| Future districts | Via Ed Advance (Matt Mervis), Roche's superintendent network | Not started | After 2-3 case studies exist | Q3-Q4 2026 |

**The Jeff Carlson playbook (validated April 6):**
- Get to the tech team early. It signals partnership, not circumvention.
- Target the middle third of teachers. Early adopters are already sold. Win the skeptical-but-open group and you have 2/3 of the building.
- Get to ~12 pilot sites across different types (public, private, charter, different sizes) before scaling.
- Danbury is big enough that people in the region know it. Strong anchor.
- Many school contracts are public record. Track what competitors charge.

---

### Pipeline 3: Side Projects (Building for Trusted People)

**What we sell:** Custom AI tools and automations for people in our network. Small projects. Learning by building.

**Who buys it:** People who already trust us. Friends, mentors, family connections, professionals in our circle.

**Why it matters:**
- Every project builds our portfolio and sharpens our skills
- Follows the Applied AI playbook: "Your first clients are not strangers. They are people who already know you, respect you, and would take your call."
- Low pressure, high learning. These conversations don't need to lead to district deals or sovereign OS sales.
- Some of these evolve into real recurring clients. Jeff Carlson started as a knowledge source and now has a proposal page.

**Current pipeline:**

| Person | Connection | Status | Next Move |
|--------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Jeff Carlson | Clever, cold email → call | Proposal page live | Send link from work email |
| Rish Pathak | Via Brian B, AAFMAA | Draft email ready | Send, get on a call |
| Brian B | Direct | Need to call | Ask who in his network needs AI help |
| Mom's boss | Family | On to-do list | Get details from mom |
| Emmanuel Juan | 72 Architects | Keep warm | Reach out when specific idea emerges |

**Pricing:** Flexible. Some free (for learning), some at $500-2,000 (for portfolio), some evolve into retainers.

---

### Not a Pipeline: Relationship Building

Some people are not prospects. They are the network. They open doors, provide intel, give credibility, and keep us grounded. Never sell to them. Just stay connected.

| Person | Why They Matter | Cadence |
|--------|----------------|---------|
| Pannone | Advisor, Wooster insider, 4-month champion | Monthly in-person, ongoing text |

4. Start building the language+content prototype

### Phase 2: Deliver and Prove (May-June)
**Revenue target: $30,000-60,000 cumulative**

| Action | Pipeline | Revenue |
|--------|----------|---------|
| Roche's Jarvis is running. Document before/after. Film testimonial. | Sovereign OS | $2,500 (already signed) |

**Key milestone:** By end of June, we have 3+ sovereign OS clients running, 1 district engagement active, and 2+ side projects completed. The skills library is 80% built. New clients take 1 day to set up instead of 3.

**Brayan's job this phase:** Sell the next wave using Roche's case study. Deliver training sessions.

**Ryan's job this phase:** Build and deliver. Roche's Jarvis infrastructure. Byrnes AI framework. Language+content prototype. Template everything so client #4 is faster than client #1.

### Phase 3: Scale Through Networks (July-September)

**Key milestone:** Recurring monthly revenue from retainers. Ryan is delivering independently. The product is no longer Brayan-dependent for setup.

### Phase 4: Productize and Expand (October-December)
**Revenue target: $300,000-500,000 cumulative**

| 2-3 district contracts active. Language+content tool is proven. | District | $50,000-100,000 |
| Begin packaging language+content as licensable software (not consulting) | District | Future $1M+ market |
| Expand beyond CT: NY, MA districts through network referrals | District | New markets |
| Hire first employee or contractor for delivery | All | Scale |

---

## How We Measure Progress

**We do NOT measure hours worked.** Input measurement is a productivity trap. You can spend 12 hours building a prettier website and feel productive while zero pipeline movement happens.

**We measure pipeline progression:**

| Stage | Definition | Metric |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| Conversation | We've talked to them | Count of active conversations |
| Pain Identified | We know their specific problem and can quantify it | Count with quantified pain |
| Proposal Sent | They have a scope and number in front of them | Count of outstanding proposals |
| Signed | Money committed or contract agreed | Revenue signed |
| Delivered | Value proven, system running | Client satisfaction + testimonial |
| Referral | They send us the next person | Count of referrals generated |

**Weekly check:** How many people moved from one stage to the next this week? That's the number that matters.

**Monthly check:** Revenue signed. Revenue collected. Pipeline value (total $ in proposal stage).

---

## The Competitive Moat

Why nobody else can do what we do:

1. **We are practitioners.** We built our own Jarvis and run our entire business on it. No competitor demonstrates their product by showing you their own daily workflow. We do.

2. **The sovereign model has no competitor.** Every AI tool trains on your data. Ours doesn't. For leaders handling sensitive information (student data, board negotiations, personnel decisions), we are the only safe option.

3. **We build live.** No pitch deck. No mockup. We build a working app in 5 minutes in front of 50 educators. Nobody else can do that.

4. **We are 18.** That sounds like a weakness. It is our greatest asset. Teachers compared us to Harvard professors and The Breakthrough Coach and preferred us. "He's a real person." Our age means we have no corporate baggage, no buzzwords, no PowerPoint culture. We show up and build.

5. **We are in the building.** We don't sell from a website. We walk the halls. We know the teachers by name. We know Roche stayed after school to throw a football with a kid. We know Pannone introduced AI at Wooster. We know Williams needs a report card comment generator. Nobody else has this depth of relationship.

6. **The language+content use case.** When this product works, we own a market that doesn't exist yet. Every diverse district in America needs this. Current tools only support Spanish. We support 90+ languages. This is the $10M+ product.

---

## The Roles

**Brayan: Sell, Relate, Lead**
- Every sales conversation
- Every relationship touchpoint
- Every in-person meeting and demo
- Strategic direction and pricing decisions
- Training and onboarding clients
- Public speaking and PD delivery

**Ryan: Build, Deliver, Scale**
- Jarvis infrastructure and templates
- Custom tool development (report card generator, language+content tool)
- Website and platform maintenance
- Client system setup (once productized)
- Technical documentation

**The split is clear: Brayan sells, Ryan builds. They converge on delivery.**

---

## The North Star

**"I give leaders back the hours that matter and the capabilities they didn't know they could have, so they can lead instead of manage."**

For superintendents: lead instead of manage. Visit classrooms. Be at your son's game.
For teachers: teach instead of drown in admin. Focus on the student in front of you.

The product is the sovereign OS. The delivery is trust-first relationships. The revenue model is setup + retainer. The competitive moat is that we are practitioners who build live and keep data sovereign.

**Send the proposal. Close the deal. Build the system. Get the case study. Double the price. Repeat.**

---

*"I don't build things that people don't need. And I don't build things people won't buy. Because in order for this to be sustainable, people need to buy. There needs to be funding for it."*
